elements.sent-2411-005: We review the main processes that drive the morphodynamics of dunes, i.e. their growth in height, migration and elongation, and emphasise the contribution of experiments to the understanding of these mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-006: The main control parameters are the sediment flux $Q$ and the saturation length $L_{\rm sat}$ associated with the spatial relaxation of the flux towards the transport capacity.
elements.sent-2411-007: The other relevant quantities are essentially dimensionless: fluid response to a bed perturbation, dune geometry (orientation, aspect ratio), transport ratios under multi-directional wind regimes.
elements.sent-2411-008: We argue that laboratory experiments dealing with sedimentary bedforms in water flows are good analogues to study the morphodynamics of aeolian dunes at reduced length and time scales, as $L_{\rm sat}$ and $L_{\rm sat ^2/Q$ are expected to be smaller for subaqueous bedload.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Besides, dune shape and dynamics are mainly governed by flow and boundary conditions, independent of the transport mode.
elements.sent-2411-010: We discuss different experimental set-ups and results, especially concerning dune pattern orientation and dune interaction.
elements.sent-2411-011: Under natural wind regimes in terrestrial deserts, we show the potential of field experiments in which the control of initial and boundary conditions allows for the quantification of all the relevant mechanisms involved in dune growth.
elements.sent-2411-012: We emphasise the general agreement between observations, measurements and theoretical predictions, which indicates a robust comprehension of the underlying processes.
elements.sent-2411-013: This understanding can serve as a foundation for further investigations, including the interpretation of dune landscapes and the resolution of inverse problems.
elements.sent-2411-014: Aeolian sand dunes display magnificent sedimentary patterns in various environments \cite{Lore14,Dini17,Gunn22 : terrestrial deserts \cite{Bree79 and coastal areas \cite{Hesp13a , but also planetary surfaces such as those of Mars \cite{Cutt73,Bour10,Lapo18 , Titan \cite{Lore06,Rade08,Rodr13 , Venus \cite{Gree84, Basi85, Weit94 , Pluto \cite{Telf18 and comet 67P \cite{Jia17 .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: The shape, size and orientation of these dunes are determined by various factors, including the prevailing wind regime and the availability of sediment, as well as the hydrodynamic regimes \cite{Cour24 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: On Earth, individual dunes span length scales from 10 to $10^3\;{\rm m}$, and they can interact with each other in dune fields of hundreds of kilometres \cite{Bree79 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Small dunes emerge or react quickly to consistent winds over a few days, while large dunes and dune fields integrate wind regimes over long times.
elements.sent-2411-018: This integrative behaviour allows for a rich variety of shapes and makes it difficult to interpret a dune landscape in all its complexity.
elements.sent-2411-019: Dunes are thus witnesses of past winds and they have been used to constrain global climate models on the planetary bodies where they are observed.
elements.sent-2411-020: Performing comparative planetology in dune geomorphology is also a challenge due to the significant variability in environmental parameters.

elements.sent-2411-021: To study the fundamental processes at work in dune dynamics, field observations take advantage of remote sensing to explore vast areas, but they are limited to a narrow range of time scales and often lack of sufficiently idealised configurations to specify the initial condition or separate coupled effects.
elements.sent-2411-022: Here we discuss the contribution of experiments in the understanding of dune pattern development, and this paper is constructed as follows.
elements.sent-2411-023: A first theoretical section briefly reviews the growing mode in height associated with a bed instability, and an elongation process in multidirectional wind regimes (Sec.~\ref{theory ).}
elements.sent-2411-024: Section 3 describes laboratory experiments, where, in order to cut down space and time scales of aeolian transport processes, dune dynamics is analogously studied in water tanks and channels, where the grains are moved by a water flow.
elements.sent-2411-025: Section 4 is devoted to landscape-scale experiments, i.e., dealing in the field with actual space and time scales of the dune under controlled initial and boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-026: Importantly, sedimentary bedforms also naturally develop in subaqueous environments, such as rivers and sea floors.
elements.sent-2411-027: Although we do exploit here the (partial) analogy between aeolian and subaqueous sediment transport, we will not review the vast literature on sedimentary subaqueous pattern, which includes not only ripples and dunes, but also other types like anti-dunes, chevrons and bars associated with specific geometries and regimes of free-surface channelised water flows \cite{Best05, Carl00, Pars05, Iked83, Cole94, Cole96, Cole03, Baas94, Vend05, Lang07, Ouri09, Chan71, Lisl91, Lanz00, Deva10, Rodr14 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Furthermore, we will not address in detail the sensitivity of sediment transport to environmental parameters (e.g., fluid and grain density, grain size distribution).
elements.sent-2411-029: Instead, we will abstract sediment transport into a few key quantities that control the main features of dune development in all these different environments.
elements.sent-2411-030: Finally, we will not consider cohesion between grains, nor the effect of vegetation.
elements.sent-2411-031: These aspects are mentioned in the conclusion as perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-032: In order to present the general framework of dune pattern formation, it is first necessary to introduce the dimensionless numbers that concern sediment transport by a fluid flow over a granular bed \cite{Dura11a, Vala15, Paht20 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: We then review the instability mechanism by which dunes can emerge from a flat bed under a unidirectional flow \cite{Andr02, Kroy02a, Kroy02b, Clau06, Nart09, Char13, Cour15 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: We finally discuss the case of a multi-directional flow regime, especially where dunes can elongate over a consolidated substratum \cite{Cour14, Gao15a, Luca15, Rozi19 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: These theoretical considerations serve as a basis for the development and interpretation of the experiments, both in the laboratory and in the field.
elements.sent-2411-036: A fluid flow over a granular surface can set the grains into motion when the shear stress it exerts on the bed exceeds a certain threshold.
elements.sent-2411-037: Three main dimensionless parameters controlling the corresponding sediment transport can be built \cite{Dura12 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Considering grains of bulk density $\rho_p$ and diameter $d$ in a fluid of density $\rho_f$ and kinematic viscosity $\nu$, we define the density ratio $s$, the Shields number $\Theta$ to rescale the bed shear stress $\rho_f u_*^2$, and the Galileo number $\mathcal{G}$ to rescale the viscosity.
elements.sent-2411-039: They write: \begin{equation}s = \rho_p/\rho_f, \qquad \Theta = u_*^2 / u_g^2 \, , \qquad \mathcal{G} = u_g d / \nu \, , \label{sThetamathcalG}\end{equation} where $g$ is the gravity acceleration, and $u_g = \sqrt{(s -1) gd}$ a reference velocity scale.
elements.sent-2411-040: As shown by numerical simulations \cite{Dura12 , $s$ essentially governs the transition from bedload ($s$ of the order of a few units, the moving grains keep contact with the bed) to saltation ($s$ larger than a few tens, the grains experience jumps and make dissipative rebounds when colliding with the bed).}

elements.subsection-2411-007: Dunes in multidirectional flows and different sand supply conditions - the two dune growth mechanisms
elements.sent-2411-177: The relationship between wind regimes and dune type, shape and orientation is at the heart of dune studies, not least because dunes and their deposits are proxies for past winds.
elements.sent-2411-178: Most experimental studies focus on understanding equilibrium shapes and how dunes respond to changes in wind regime or sand supply conditions, the two main factors controlling free dune type (free dunes are dunes for which wind and sand transport are not constrained by other factors such as obstacles or vegetation \cite{Pye90, Cook93, Livi96,Cour24 ).}
elements.sent-2411-179: Here we discuss a selection of experiments, which together with numerical simulations ({\it{e.g. , \cite{Part09, Nart09, Dura10, Zhan12 ), have shown that dune shape is controlled by its dynamics, which can be decomposed in the three processes of growth in height, migration and elongation \cite{Cour24 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-180: The relative balance of these three dynamics is mainly controlled by flow regime and sediment supply and availability \cite{Cour24 .}

elements.sent-2411-197: Dunes highly interact with their environment.
elements.sent-2411-198: Dune interactions can be divided into three categories: dune interaction with obstacles, dune interaction with each other, and dune interaction with vegetation.
elements.sent-2411-199: Experiments are particularly important for dune interactions because numerical models of dunes only partially reproduce the complexity of the flow and, in particular, the wake that develops downwind of the dune.
elements.sent-2411-200: Barchan interactions with obstacles has recently been investigated by Assis {\it{et al. (Fig.~\ref{FigInteract , \cite{Assi23 ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-201: In their study obstacles have sizes comparable to the size of dunes.
elements.sent-2411-202: They have shown that depending on the different aspect ratios between the dune and the obstacle, the different aspect ratios of the obstacle, and the flow velocity, the dune will either pass over the obstacle, bypass the obstacle, or get stuck and form an echo dune.
elements.sent-2411-203: During the process, sand may be trapped in the lee side of the obstacle and form a lee (or shadow) dune.
elements.sent-2411-204: These studies are particularly important for risk prevention and management of human infrastructure in desert environments.
elements.sent-2411-205: Under a unidirectional wind, dunes on starved beds are migrating barchan dunes.

elements.sent-2411-209: An isolated barchan should either shrink or grow.
elements.sent-2411-210: In deserts, barchan are not isolated, but are numerous and interact (Fig.~\ref{FigDunes d).}
elements.sent-2411-211: Their interactions appear to regulate their size and their arrangement within the field \cite{Bagn41, Elbe05 .}
elements.sent-2411-212: Two types of interactions have been identified corresponding to long-range and short-range sand exchange.
elements.sent-2411-213: Experiments have shown that dune collisions can result in dune merging, sand redistribution between the two dunes, or formation of new dunes, depending on the relative size and alignment between impacting and impacted dunes (Fig.~\ref{FigInteract , \cite{Endo04, Hers05, Assi20 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-214: Dunes merge if the impacting dune is much smaller than the impacted one.
elements.sent-2411-215: If the impacting dune is larger, the flow perturbation downstream the impacting dune locally increases the fluid velocity experienced by the impacted dune enough to cut it into pieces.
elements.sent-2411-216: The impacting dune may merge with one or more of the newly formed dunes \cite{Endo04, Hers05 .}

elements.sent-2411-218: Following the experimental identification of collision types and parameters, a more complete phase diagram of binary collisions was developed in numerical simulations \cite{Dura09, Jarv23 .}

elements.sent-2411-220: The other important type of interaction is the interaction with vegetation.
elements.sent-2411-221: The coupling between sand transport, dunes, and vegetation is also dynamical.

elements.sent-2411-223: This coupling is still a challenge for laboratory experiments, but is of primary importance because it is relevant for most coastal deserts and for vegetated dunes that cover large areas in China and Australia.
elements.sent-2411-224: The concept of landscape-scale experiments is a novel approach to geomorphology that differs from the usual field instrumentation or remote sensing analysis, and is particularly suited to modern issues in aeolian research and dune physics.
elements.sent-2411-225: Indeed, although the minimum size of the dunes and their migration rate remain beyond the operational capacity in wind tunnels, the temporal and spatial scales associated with dune growth mechanisms appear to be accessible in the field, enabling the validation and quantification of the processes that contribute to the formation of dune patterns in their natural environment.

(b) Experimental dispersion relation for the linear regime of the dune instability (red points). elements.sent-2411-288: The interpretation of dune landscapes, which result from complex interactions (superimposed bedforms, collisions) and integrate wind regimes over long times, can be achieved by identifying the elementary physical processes at work and their associated scales.
elements.sent-2411-289: For this purpose, idealised and controlled experiments, both at laboratory and landscape scales, are an invaluable source of knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-290: A comprehensive range of dunes, encompassing a multitude of shapes and orientations, can be replicated and tracked over time, from their emergence to their interactions and coarsening dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-291: These experiments are also key to assess the models with which these processes are theoretically described, and to quantify their relevant parameters.
elements.sent-2411-292: In turn, the theory provides a framework for understanding the main controlling scales, in particular by analysing the extent to which the processes at work in the morphodynamics of subaqueous laboratory experiments dealing with bedload and centimetre-scale sedimentary bedforms can be analogous to those of large aeolian sand dunes generated by saltation.
elements.sent-2411-293: This approach of dune dynamics based on processes and scales also gives a quantitative reference and building blocks for numerical models, such as agent-based and cellular automata, to simulate larger dune fields.
elements.sent-2411-294: In particular setting the values of the saturation length $L_{\rm sat}$ and the sand flux $Q$ allows for a quantitative calibration of the results \cite{Nart09 .}
elements.sent-2411-295: It also lays the physical foundation for the comparison of dunes in terrestrial and planetary environments \cite{Bour10,Lore14,Luca14,Jia17,Day18,Lapo18,Dura19,Cour24 , for which fluid and sediment characteristics can take values significantly different than those commonly in terrestrial deserts.}
elements.sent-2411-296: Given the central role of the saturation length in dune dynamics, further experiments are required to investigate how $L_{\rm sat}$ depends on environmental parameters, and to relate it to the properties of the fluid and the grains, the flow regime, and the structure of the transport layer.
elements.sent-2411-297: In particular, low pressure conditions (larges values of $s$, the density ratio between the particles and the gas), which are typical of planetary surfaces, must be further explored \cite{Andr21 .}
elements.sent-2411-298: In this limit, one expects a smooth hydrodynamic regime to emerge associated with enhanced values of kinematic viscosity as well as a reduced drag force on the grains \cite{Jia17, Dura19, Cour24 .}
elements.sent-2411-299: The same is true for the sediment flux $Q$ and the transport threshold $u_t$.
elements.sent-2411-300: This knowledge is essential for using these dune patterns as observational tools to learn more about the flow and sediment properties in places where direct measurements are often difficult to implement.
elements.sent-2411-301: Planetary environments as diverse as those reported on Mars and Titan will thus continue to provide a wide range of dune patterns that will undoubtedly serve to guide the development of new experiments, as well as provide empirical constraints to improve the theoretical parametrisation and description of the processes.

elements.sent-2411-303: These primary and secondary structures are valuable sources of information on the properties of surface winds over different periods, from seasonal to millennial time scales.
elements.sent-2411-304: In areas where sediment availability varies spatially, the different growth mechanisms can also coexist \cite{Lu17,Lu22 , or the same mechanism can produce dunes with different orientations \cite{Zhan12 .}}
elements.sent-2411-305: Experiments have not yet addressed superimposed patterns and complex shapes such as star dunes or raked dunes.
elements.sent-2411-306: Further long-term experimental studies are required to investigate dune interaction and coarsening processes in this context of superimposed patterns or for the spatial organisation of dune fields, such as barchan corridors \cite{Elbe08 .}
elements.sent-2411-307: Of particular interest is a better understanding of the dynamics by which dune fields eliminate or create defects in the dune pattern, in order to quantify how far from a possible equilibrium state the dune system is \cite{Day18,Marv23 .}

elements.sent-2411-309: Finally, dealing with vegetation is an important step for laboratory and landscape scale experiments dedicated to dune dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-310: Coastal zone studies and management over the last few decades have generated a vast amount of data and observations, which have yet to be fully exploited \cite{Cast19 .}
elements.sent-2411-311: All this information offers significant potential for the development of new field experiments under controlled conditions in the presence of vegetation \cite{Lapo21 .}
elements.sent-2411-312: Meanwhile, growing plants in the laboratory is a difficult time consuming task, and it remains to be determined what the best experimental analogue of vegetation is.
elements.sent-2411-313: A primary objective would be to ascertain some mechanical properties of plants that are relevant to sediment transport and dune morphodynamics.
elements.sent-2411-314: All these experimental efforts should be combined with studies investigating the role of cohesion (e.g., moisture, fine material, sintering, but also coupling to icy surfaces as on Mars), which introduces additional sources of interaction between bedforms, and can modify shear stress and sediment transport, eventually leading to possibly similar feedbacks of those involved by vegetation \cite{Rala22 .}
